chunk.text: ith EURUSD. • This breakdown of the correlation between gold and EURUSD over the last two years has seen periods of normal positive correlation (e.g. as the Dollar rises, gold falls) and periods of negative correlation when gold and the Dollar move in the same direc- tion. These latter periods have coincided with times of market stress as shown by the VIX index. • Looking into 2012, we expect the Euro crisis to con- tinue to be acute in Q1 and probably Q2. Although we do not expect quick or convincing solutions to be found to the Euro crisis, we expect the Euro to survive and for risk to slowly disappear. This means that market stress (e.g. VIX) could peak again in H1 but should be lower in H2 2012. • If this occurs, then the normal negative correlation with the Dollar should resume in H2 but a strong Dollar in H1 could lead to stronger gold prices. Sil- ver is likely to underperform in H1 due to this corre- lation but should perform better in price terms in H2. Price correlations Links between currency movements and precious metal prices. Mitsui Global Precious Metals

chunk.text: 2 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Price correlations 20 January 2012 Traditionally, most commodities are priced and traded in US Dollars. As the US Dollar strengthens against other currencies, any commodity naturally becomes more expensive in those other currencies and has to weaken in USD terms to reach equilibrium. So, in the absence of any other driving forces, a stronger Dollar would tend to mean that commodity prices should fall. Other factors including fundamental supply and demand will influence price movements. As can be seen from Figure 1, this means that there should be a negative correlation be- tween commodity prices and the US Dollar (or a positive correlation between the Euro and commodity prices). This correlation will typically be much lower than 1 as commodity- specific fundamentals and investor attitudes play a part in moving prices. Plotting prices against the strength of the US Dollar necessarily requires the choice of an- other currency to compare the USD with. Typically this is either the Euro or DXY. DXY itself is a trade-weighted currency but, unfortunately the weightings related to the volume of trade carried out thirty years ago with specific countries.

relations.evidence: any commodity naturally becomes more expensive in those other currencies and has to weaken in USD terms
positive correlation between the Euro and commodity prices

chunk.text: f is a trade-weighted currency but, unfortunately the weightings related to the volume of trade carried out thirty years ago with specific countries. For most trading, the most im- portant currency is the Euro since this is the second largest (free-floating) reserve cur- rency after the Dollar itself. However, the link with other currencies can also be examined. We choose to use two currency pairs to examine correlations between US Dollars and the gold price: EURUSD is the currency pair which normally corresponds well to gold move- ments. However, looking back over the past ten years, there is a very similar correlation between CADUSD (the Canadian Dollar in US Dollar terms) and gold in USD terms. Until 2009, we feel that most currency movements were based on conventional measures of the strength of each currency. Since the USD is effectively the world’s global currency, its strength or weakness was the dominant factor and the correlation with EURUSD or CA- DUSD was very similar. Which currency?

chunk.text: 3 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Price correlations 20 January 2012 Since 2009, the European debt crisis has had a strong impact on the foreign exchange markets. With political risks increasing, the relationship between currencies has been based less on interest rates, inflation or bond yields than on the prospect of sovereign de- fault and the desire to own a safe haven. Looking over the shorter term (the past two years), it is clear that the normal relationship between gold prices and EURUSD has broken down with a correlation coefficient very close to zero. In contrast, the relationship between gold and CADUSD has been much stronger. We interpret the EURUSD relationship as fear-driven with many investors look- ing to exit the Euro purely out of concern for its stability with money flowing into the US Dollar instead. At times when these flows are large, the urge to buy gold as a safe haven is high. When these flows stop, some of this “insurance” gold is sold. However, move- ments between the Canadian and US Dollars represent something closer to pure Dollar weakness or strength and show a more normal relationship between currency movements and commodity prices.

relations.evidence: the relationship between gold prices and EURUSD has broken down with a correlation coefficient very close to zero
the relationship between gold and CADUSD has been much stronger

chunk.text: s represent something closer to pure Dollar weakness or strength and show a more normal relationship between currency movements and commodity prices. The breakdown of the EURUSD relationship to the gold prices is therefore driven by worries over the Euro. chunk.text: 4 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Price correlations 20 January 2012 Euro weakness has been one driving force for gold investment over the last eighteen months. However, there have also been moments of Dollar weakness (March to May 2011) where the correlation has broken down. What is clear, however, is that the correla- tion between gold and EURUSD has broken down on moments of extreme stress in the market, as shown by a rising VIX index (which measures volatility of equities). chunk.text: 5 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Price correlations 20 January 2012 Looking at the relationship between gold and EURUSD and that between silver and EU- RUSD over the past decade, the correlation coefficients are quite similar. However, over the last two years, there is a clear difference between the behaviour of the two metals. Gold has shown little correlation to movements in EURUSD while silver has retained a stronger link. We believe this is because of gold’s stronger “safe haven” credentials with gold attracting flows of capital at times of market stress and seeing outflows at times of lower risk. The underlying relationship between gold and silver ensures that silver does gain from some of gold’s attraction (the correlation to EURUSD is higher than for gold but lower than for aluminium for instance). chunk.text: lations 20 January 2012 Over the shorter term, the Euro looks likely to continue to weaken. No convincing plan exists yet to solve the debt problems. In the near term, Italy and Spain will have to roll over significant amounts of sovereign debt and this will add to market stresses. Looking slightly further ahead, Greece is in imminent danger of default and this should lead to a still weaker Euro. Both of these challenges should also lead to higher risk (e.g. VIX) and therefore to a weaker correlation between the Dollar and the gold price. This equates to flat or stronger gold prices in Q1 but to potentially weaker silver prices. We do, however, expect some progress to be made in “curing” the Euro’s problems and we expect the Euro to survive. This should lead to a gradual reduction in the perception of market risk and may lead to a stronger Euro. We would expect to see a higher correlation between EURUSD and the gold price (in USD) in H2 and higher gold prices (in USD). Sil- ver prices should benefit too. Outlook Figure 12: Technical and fundamental analysis suggests that the Euro will continue to decline against the Dollar in the short term.

relations.evidence: The Euro will continue to decline against the Dollar in the short term.
